case_name: Trichloroethylene removal from contaminated surface water for municipal drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for trichloroethylene-contaminated surface water intended for drinking water supply under a preference for proven technologies.

input_data.scenario_background: A municipal utility draws from a small river-reservoir system impacted by an upstream legacy solvent plume, with trichloroethylene detected consistently in the raw water. The plant must continue producing drinking water and prefers established, well-documented treatment approaches rather than emerging technologies. Surface water quality varies seasonally, so the selected treatment train should handle both volatile organic compound removal and routine particulate and microbial control.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should reliably comply with drinking water limits for trichloroethylene and meet normal drinking water quality expectations for turbidity and microbiological safety.
secondary_goals: use proven and widely accepted technology
maintain robust performance under seasonal surface water variability
avoid unnecessarily high energy or specialty operation
limit formation of avoidable taste and odor or disinfection by-product issues

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: conventional solids residuals can be managed and off-gas treatment is feasible if needed
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: stable year-round operation with conventional utility staffing is required

core_unit_functions.coagulation: destabilize colloids and reduce particulate and natural organic matter load
core_unit_functions.flocculation: form settleable flocs for solids removal
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: remove bulk suspended solids before downstream VOC treatment
core_unit_functions.filtration: polish turbidity and protect downstream units
core_unit_functions.air_stripping: transfer volatile trichloroethylene from water to air for effective removal
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbiological control for drinking water supply

applicability_rationale: trichloroethylene is a volatile organic compound for which air stripping is a well-established and proven removal method
surface water matrix requires conventional clarification and filtration ahead of final drinking water production
the utility preference for proven technology strongly favors conventional treatment paired with established VOC stripping
moderate capex and opex constraints are compatible with a standard clarification-filtration train plus stripping tower
potential_risks: off-gas from air stripping may require vapor-phase treatment or regulatory management
cold water temperatures or inadequate air-to-water ratio can reduce stripping efficiency
upstream turbidity spikes can impair overall plant stability if solids removal is not well controlled

core_unit_functions.coagulation: reduce suspended solids and part of natural organic matter
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: settle flocs and reduce solids loading
core_unit_functions.filtration: provide turbidity polishing and protect carbon adsorbers
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb trichloroethylene and provide polishing for trace organics
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished water

applicability_rationale: granular activated carbon is a proven technology for removal of many synthetic organic chemicals including trichloroethylene
pretreatment is important because surface water DOC and turbidity can otherwise shorten carbon bed life
this approach is robust and familiar to many drinking water utilities
it avoids air emissions management associated with stripping and can also provide broader organic polishing
potential_risks: natural organic matter competes for adsorption sites and can increase carbon replacement frequency
breakthrough monitoring is essential to avoid loss of TCE removal performance
spent carbon handling and regeneration logistics are required

core_unit_functions.coagulation: remove colloids and reduce organic fouling potential
core_unit_functions.flocculation: build larger flocs for solids separation
core_unit_functions.air_stripping: remove the bulk of volatile trichloroethylene
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: provide polishing for residual organics and breakthrough protection

applicability_rationale: combined stripping and GAC is highly protective and technically strong for TCE control
the configuration is useful where very stable low finished-water concentrations are desired
conventional surface water treatment remains necessary because of raw water turbidity and microbial concerns
potential_risks: more unit processes increase operational burden relative to using either stripping or GAC alone
both off-gas management and carbon replacement may be needed
the added polishing may be unnecessary for a straightforward moderate-TCE case

core_unit_functions.membrane_filtration_for_turbidity_contro: remove suspended solids and microorganisms from surface water
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb trichloroethylene and other trace organics
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final pathogen inactivation or residual maintenance

applicability_rationale: this can be technically feasible where compact solids removal is preferred
GAC remains the main proven barrier for TCE in this train
membrane pretreatment can stabilize turbidity before adsorption
potential_risks: membrane filtration does not directly remove dissolved TCE and therefore relies on downstream GAC
higher operational sophistication is needed than for conventional clarification
membrane fouling and cleaning requirements may not align with a simple proven-technology preference

core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust corrosivity and finished water chemistry after desalting treatment

applicability_rationale: reverse osmosis can reduce a wide range of contaminants, including chlorinated organics to some extent
it may be considered only when multiple dissolved contaminants require simultaneous high-level control
potential_risks: unnecessarily complex and energy intensive for a primary TCE case where air stripping or GAC are established options
concentrate management creates a significant residuals burden
surface water pretreatment requirements would still be substantial

preferred_rank_pattern: conventional_surface_water_treatment_plus_air_stripping should rank first or second for trichloroethylene because TCE is volatile and stripping is well proven
granular_activated_carbon with appropriate pretreatment should rank near the top as an established alternative for chlorinated solvent removal
high-pressure membrane options should not rank first under a proven-technology and moderate-constraint scenario

must_reflect: preference for established municipal treatment technologies
need to address both surface water particulates and dissolved trichloroethylene
avoidance of unnecessarily complex or high-energy solutions when simpler proven methods exist

must_contain: why volatility of trichloroethylene supports air stripping
why surface water matrix effects such as turbidity and DOC make pretreatment important
why granular activated carbon is a credible proven alternative
why reverse osmosis ranks lower for this straightforward VOC control case

should_mention: DOC competition and breakthrough concerns for activated carbon
seasonal temperature or raw water variability affecting performance
